context.text: Which exam are you preparing for?
When do you expect to take your exam?
Which part feels hardest for you right now?

items.text: I can introduce myself in Dutch and say where I live, where I'm from, and what I do.
I can describe a simple picture or situation in Dutch using 3–4 full sentences.
I can understand the main instruction in a short Dutch appointment message or voicemail.
I can follow a short, slow Dutch conversation about everyday topics (work, shopping, health).
I can understand a short letter from my gemeente, school, or huisarts.
I can find specific information (a date, a price, a time) in a Dutch announcement or schedule.
I can write a short note in Dutch, for example to report my child sick at school.
I can fill in a simple Dutch form with my personal details without help.
I know what to do in common Dutch situations, like registering with a huisarts or using DigiD.
I understand common Dutch customs at work and school, like appointments and being on time.

diagnostic.text: Read the message: “Beste ouders, woensdag 12 maart is de school gesloten. De kinderen zijn vrij.” — What does this message tell you?

options.text: The school is closed on Wednesday 12 March
The children must come to school earlier
There is a parents' evening on 12 March
The school is looking for volunteers

diagnostic.text: You read: “De apotheek is open van maandag tot vrijdag van 8.30 tot 17.30 uur. Zaterdag alleen open tot 12.00 uur.” — When can you collect medicine on Saturday?

options.text: Not at all, it is closed

diagnostic.text: At the gemeente you hear: “U moet eerst een nummertje trekken en dan wachten tot u aan de beurt bent.” — What should you do first?
A colleague says: “Kun je morgen een uurtje eerder komen? We hebben een teamoverleg.” — What is the best response?

options.text: Ik ben gisteren naar de markt gegaan.
Ik ben gisteren naar de markt gaan.
Ik gisteren ben naar de markt gegaan.
Ik ben naar gisteren de markt gegaan.

diagnostic.text: You are ill and cannot go to work. What is the normal thing to do in the Netherlands?

options.text: Call your employer in the morning to report sick (ziekmelden)
Say nothing and explain later in the week
Ask a family member to visit your workplace
Go to the hospital first for a doctor's note

diagnostic.text: You need to see a medical specialist. How does this usually work in the Netherlands?

options.text: You go directly to the hospital and ask for a specialist
You first visit your huisarts, who can refer you (verwijzing)
You call 112 to make an appointment
You ask the apotheek for a referral

speakingNote: Speaking is scored from self-assessment only in this version; the RateMyDutch app records and grades real speech with the official rubric.

bands.label: Early preparation
bands.summary: You're at the start of your preparation. That's a fine place to be — you now know exactly where you stand. Focus on building daily contact with Dutch before drilling exam tasks.

bands.label: Building readiness
bands.summary: You have a real foundation. Some skills are close to exam level and others need focused work. Concentrated practice on your weakest skill will move your result fastest.

bands.label: Practice-ready
bands.summary: You're close. At this level the biggest gains come from realistic exam practice under exam conditions — timed tasks, one-time audio, and honest feedback on your speaking and writing.

speaking.label: Spreken (Speaking)
speaking.why: Speaking is the skill most candidates under-practise because it's hard to practise alone — and it's graded live on adequacy, vocabulary, grammar, fluency and pronunciation.
exercises: Record yourself answering one everyday question in Dutch each day (60 seconds), then listen back once.
Describe a photo out loud in 4 full sentences: who, what, where, why.
Practise your self-introduction until you can say it without pausing: name, country, woonplaats, work/study, family.
speaking.appAction: Use the Spreken section in RateMyDutch: record your answer, get it transcribed, and receive a score on the official 5-part rubric.

listening.label: Luisteren (Listening)
listening.why: On the exam you hear each fragment only once, so trained one-time listening — not relistening — is the skill to build.
exercises: Listen to one short Dutch audio clip daily (jeugdjournaal, podcast at slow speed) without pausing, then summarise it in one sentence.
Practise catching numbers, times and dates: write down every one you hear in a clip.
Listen to a voicemail-style message and answer: who is calling, what do they want, what must I do?
listening.appAction: Use the Luisteren section in RateMyDutch: real one-time-playback questions that mirror the exam format.

reading.label: Lezen (Reading)
reading.why: Exam reading is about finding the right information fast in everyday texts: letters, announcements, schedules.
exercises: Read one real Dutch letter or email you've received and underline: who is it from, what do they want, is there a deadline?
Practise scanning: give yourself 60 seconds to find one specific fact (a time, a price) in an announcement.
Read a short news item on nu.nl and write down the main point in one Dutch sentence.
reading.appAction: Use the Lezen section in RateMyDutch: timed multiple-choice reading in the exam format.

writing.label: Schrijven (Writing)
writing.why: Writing is graded strictly on execution, grammar, spelling, clarity and vocabulary — small recurring errors cost predictable points.
exercises: Write one short practical note per day: report sick, make an appointment, reply to an invitation (3–4 sentences).
Rewrite yesterday's note fixing one thing: word order, capital letters, or verb forms.
Practise filling in a form with your details: naam, adres, geboortedatum, handtekening.
writing.appAction: Use the Schrijven section in RateMyDutch: write a task and get graded feedback on the official DUO rubric.

knowledge.label: KNM (Dutch society)
knowledge.why: KNM is the most learnable section — it tests fixed knowledge about health care, work, school and government that you can simply study.
exercises: Learn one KNM theme per day: zorg, werk, onderwijs, wonen, geschiedenis, overheid.
For each theme, write down the 3 practical rules (e.g. huisarts first, then specialist).
Quiz yourself: what would you do in this situation? Answer out loud in Dutch.
knowledge.appAction: Use the KNM section in RateMyDutch: scenario questions across all 6 official themes.

disclaimer: RateMyDutch is an independent practice tool. It is not affiliated with DUO or the Dutch government, and this scorecard does not predict your official exam result.
